figures.caption: Table 2: Comparison of MemCog with Memory-as-Tool methods on LongMemEval. The QA and LLM-as-Judge configurations follow (Wu et al., 2025). All experiments in this table use GPT-4o-judged systems. Baseline results are sourced from (Latimer et al., 2025) and (Sen et al., 2026).

Table 3: Comparison of MemCog with Memory-as-Tool methods on ProactiveMemBench. QA and metric configurations are detailed in Appendices B.3–B.5. All experiments in this table use GLM-5.1 as the backbone. All baselines are reproduced using their official implementations.
Figure 2: Example of MemCog: Proactive Reasoning Protocol: Navigable Memory Store and Cross-Dimensional Navigation path examples.

Figure 3: New pages created per session as conversations progress. Left: grouped by every 5 sessions; Right: grouped by 10% conversation progress percentile. Both views exhibit a consistent declining trend with negative slope, indicating sub-linear page growth.
Table 8: Navigation strategies across query complexity levels. Each category illustrates how the agent adapts its navigation depth and tool selection to the task’s demands.

Table 1: Comparison of MemCog with Memory-as-Tool methods on the LoCoMo benchmark. The QA and LLM-as-Judge configurations follow (Chhikara et al., 2025). † indicates results sourced from (Du et al., 2025) and (Yue et al., 2026); all other baselines are reproduced using their official implementations.
